questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: What is the primary purpose of piping in a power plant?

options.text: To transport fluids from one piece of equipment to another
To store fluids for later use

questions.explanation: Piping serves as the housing to transport fluids (and sometimes fluid/solid mixtures) between equipment in power plant systems.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: Which piping material is typically used for high-temperature applications due to greater resistance to wear, fatigue, and corrosion?
questions.explanation: Low alloy steel is preferred for high-temperature applications due to its superior resistance to wear, fatigue, and corrosion.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: Which piping material is normally chosen for systems connected to the reactor because corrosion product generation must be minimized?
questions.explanation: Stainless steel is used in reactor systems where corrosion product buildup must be minimized to protect system integrity.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the five principal functions of valves?
questions.explanation: The five principal valve functions are: starting/stopping, throttling, controlling flow direction, regulating pressure, and relieving pressure. Increasing system pressure is not a valve function.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: What is the primary function of the backseat in a valve?

options.text: To remove system pressure from the packing to allow packing replacement while the system is pressurized

questions.explanation: The backseat is a critical safety feature that removes system pressure from the packing, allowing maintenance without depressurizing the system.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: Gate valves are primarily used as isolation valves rather than for throttling because:

options.text: They are more expensive than globe valves
Positioning the disk in the flow stream causes turbulence and accelerated wear

questions.explanation: Gate valves are unsuitable for throttling because disk positioning in the flow causes turbulence and accelerated wear, damaging the valve.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: Why are globe valves preferred for throttling?

options.text: They are cheaper than gate valves
The disk is beveled and seats at nearly right angles to the flow path
They operate faster than other valves

questions.explanation: Globe valves are ideal for throttling because the beveled disk seating at right angles to flow allows smooth, controlled flow regulation.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: Which statement is true about Kerotest valves?

options.text: They go from full closed to full open in approximately 1¼ turns
They are best for isolation applications only
They cannot be used for throttling
They have a packing gland design

questions.explanation: Kerotest valves are pack-less globe valves designed to go from full closed to full open in approximately 1¼ turns.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: Why should operators avoid over-torquing a Kerotest valve?

options.text: Excessive torque can crush or deform the disk, preventing the valve from opening later
It causes the valve to leak

questions.explanation: Over-torquing a Kerotest valve can crush or deform the disk, potentially making the valve impossible to open later.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: What feature makes a needle valve suitable for precise flow control?

options.text: Its long, narrow, cone-shaped stem tip

questions.explanation: A needle valve's long, narrow, cone-shaped stem tip allows fine, precise adjustment of flow rate.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: Butterfly valves are typically used in:

options.text: Large diameter, high flow, low pressure applications (e.g., circulating water)

questions.explanation: Butterfly valves are ideal for large diameter, high flow, low pressure service such as circulating water systems.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: What is the main advantage of a diaphragm valve with radioactive or corrosive fluids?

options.text: It can handle very high pressures
The fluid is completely separated from the moving parts, resulting in zero stem leakage
It operates faster than other valves

questions.explanation: Diaphragm valves completely separate the fluid from moving parts, preventing stem leakage and protecting against contamination.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: What is the primary purpose of check valves?

options.text: To allow flow in only one direction and prevent reverse flow

questions.explanation: Check valves are one-way devices that permit flow in the intended direction while preventing backflow.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: What is the key difference between a safety valve and a relief valve?

options.text: Safety valves are used for liquids, relief valves for gases
Safety valves pop fully open with little or no accumulation, while relief valves require accumulation
Relief valves pop fully open, safety valves open gradually

questions.explanation: Safety valves pop fully open at setpoint with minimal accumulation, while relief valves open gradually as pressure rises above setpoint.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: What is the primary function of a vacuum breaker?

options.text: To protect a tank or vessel from collapsing due to low internal pressure
To measure vacuum in the system

questions.explanation: A vacuum breaker is a safety device that protects vessels from structural collapse under low internal pressure conditions.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: What is the correct definition of 'Fail As Is' for valve actuators?

options.text: The valve opens when power is lost
The valve closes when power is lost
The valve remains in its current position when the actuating medium is lost
The valve springs to a safe position

questions.explanation: Fail As Is (most common in Motor Operated Valves) means the valve maintains its current position when actuating power is lost.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: Key precaution when manually operating a Motor Operated Valve (MOV):

options.text: Always tag out the power supply to the motor before using the handwheel (when feasible)
Use a wrench to increase torque application
Operate it as quickly as possible
Do not verify the position afterward

questions.explanation: Tagging out the motor power prevents inadvertent power application during manual valve operation, ensuring operator safety.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: When verifying position of a manual valve locally, operators should first attempt to turn the handwheel in which direction?

options.text: Closed direction (negligible effect if already closed)

questions.explanation: Testing closure direction is safe because if the valve is already closed, the handwheel won't move, causing no system impact.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: According to SOER 85-2, one primary method to prevent valve mispositioning is:

options.text: Verify that all local and remote indications reflect actual plant conditions before and after manipulation

questions.explanation: Verification of all indications before and after valve operation is essential to prevent dangerous mispositioning events.

questions.subject: CP01
questions.text: What is a major failure mechanism of gate valves discussed in the lesson?
questions.explanation: Thermal binding (differential cooling contraction) and pressure locking (trapped liquid expansion) are critical gate valve failure mechanisms.

questions.subject: TH01
questions.text: According to Pascal's Law, a pressure change applied to an enclosed, incompressible fluid is transmitted:

options.text: Only at the point of application
Undiminished to all points within the fluid and to the walls of the container
Only along the path of least resistance

questions.explanation: Pascal's Law: pressure applied to an enclosed fluid transmits undiminished throughout in all directions.

questions.subject: TH01
questions.text: Gauge pressure plus atmospheric pressure equals which pressure value?
questions.explanation: Pabs = Pgage + Patm. Absolute pressure is always the sum of gauge and atmospheric pressures.

questions.subject: TH01
questions.text: Specific volume is best defined as:

options.text: The total volume of a substance
The volume occupied by a unit mass of a substance (the reciprocal of density)
The volume at standard temperature and pressure

questions.explanation: Specific volume (v) = V/m, measured in ft³/lbm or m³/kg. It is the reciprocal of density.

questions.subject: TH01
questions.text: What is the primary difference between a closed system and an open system in thermodynamics?

options.text: A closed system allows energy transfer across its boundary but not mass transfer, while an open system allows transfer of both mass and energy
A closed system allows no transfer of mass or energy
An open system is always at constant pressure
A closed system is only used in laboratories

questions.explanation: Closed systems have fixed mass boundaries but allow energy exchange. Open systems (control volumes) allow both mass and energy crossing boundaries.

questions.subject: TH01
questions.text: Which type of thermodynamic property depends on the amount of substance present, such as volume or weight?
questions.explanation: Extensive properties (volume, mass, weight) depend on the amount of substance. Intensive properties (pressure, temperature) do not.

questions.subject: TH01
questions.text: On which temperature scale does zero represent the point of no molecular motion (absolute zero) and correspond exactly to zero Kelvin?
questions.explanation: The Rankine scale (absolute scale for Fahrenheit) and Kelvin (absolute scale for Celsius) both have zero at absolute zero.

questions.subject: TH01
questions.text: Which pressure measurement indicates a pressure below atmospheric pressure?
questions.explanation: Vacuum pressure is any pressure below atmospheric. Pvac = Patm - Pabs.

questions.subject: TH01
questions.text: What term describes the difference in pressure between two locations, such as across a filter?
questions.explanation: Differential pressure (psid) is the pressure difference between two points in a system, commonly used across filters and restrictors.

questions.subject: TH01
questions.text: In a hydraulic press, if the output piston has a larger area than the input piston, how does the output force compare to the input force?

options.text: The output force is less than the input force
The output force equals the input force
The output force is greater than the input force
Output force depends only on fluid type

questions.explanation: In a hydraulic press, F = P × A. A larger output piston area produces greater output force (Pascal's Law application).

questions.subject: TH01
questions.text: Why does liquid temperature have a much greater effect on density and specific volume than pressure does?

options.text: Because liquids expand more with heat
Because liquids are relatively incompressible, so pressure has little effect on their volume
Because temperature is more important than pressure

questions.explanation: Liquids are nearly incompressible, so pressure changes have minimal volume effect. Temperature changes cause greater density variations.

questions.subject: TH02
questions.text: The First Law of Thermodynamics states that energy:

options.text: Cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another
Can only flow from hot to cold
Is lost as heat in all processes

questions.explanation: The First Law (Energy Conservation): Energy entering a system equals energy stored in or leaving it. E in = E stored + E out.

questions.subject: TH02
questions.text: Heat and work are best classified as which type of energy?

options.text: Transient (energy in transit) forms of energy

questions.explanation: Heat and work are transient energy forms—energy in transit. They exist only during energy transfer between systems and surroundings.

questions.subject: TH02
questions.text: Which equation correctly defines enthalpy (H)?
questions.explanation: Enthalpy H = U + PV, where U is internal energy and PV is flow energy. This is a fundamental thermodynamic property.

questions.subject: TH02
questions.text: What is the term for energy a substance possesses due to its position or height relative to a reference point?
questions.explanation: Potential Energy (PE) depends on position. PE = mzg/gc, where z is height above a reference point.

questions.subject: TH02
questions.text: What is the term for energy a substance possesses due to its motion?
questions.explanation: Kinetic Energy (KE) = mv²/2gc. It depends on the velocity of the substance.

questions.subject: TH02
questions.text: Internal energy is best described as which of the following?

options.text: The sum of the microscopic energy forms (molecular rotation, vibration, and translation) within a substance

questions.explanation: Internal energy (U) is the sum of all microscopic forms: molecular rotation, vibration, and translational kinetic energy.

questions.subject: TH02
questions.text: Which equation correctly defines specific enthalpy (h)?
questions.explanation: Specific enthalpy h = u + Pv. It represents energy per unit mass of fluid.

questions.subject: TH02
questions.text: For a closed system with no change in potential or kinetic energy, the General Energy Equation reduces to which relationship?

options.text: Change in enthalpy = heat transferred to the system minus work done by the system (Δh = q - w)

questions.explanation: For closed systems with no PE/KE change: Δh = q - w. This is the simplified First Law energy equation.

questions.subject: TH02
questions.text: Which term describes the rate at which work is done?
questions.explanation: Power is work per unit time: P = W/t, measured in watts (J/s) or horsepower (hp).

questions.subject: TH03
questions.text: What is the term for a liquid existing at a temperature below its saturation temperature for a given pressure?
questions.explanation: Subcooled (or compressed) liquid is below saturation temperature for its pressure, meaning it could absorb heat and vaporize.

questions.subject: TH03
questions.text: What is the term for steam that is 100% vapor at its boiling temperature, with no liquid remaining?
questions.explanation: Saturated vapor (dry steam) is at saturation temperature with quality X = 100%. Any further heating produces superheated steam.

questions.subject: TH03
questions.text: What is the term for vapor that exists above its boiling (saturation) temperature?
questions.explanation: Superheated vapor is above saturation temperature. The degrees of superheat = actual T - saturation T.

questions.subject: TH03
questions.text: Heat that changes a substance's phase without changing its temperature is called:
questions.explanation: Latent heat is phase-change heat (melting, vaporization) that doesn't change temperature. Sensible heat does change temperature.

questions.subject: TH03
questions.text: What term describes the fraction of vapor present in a wet steam mixture?
questions.explanation: Steam quality (X) = fraction of vapor in wet steam mixture. Moisture content (M) = liquid fraction. X + M = 100%.

questions.subject: TH03
questions.text: On a P-T diagram, the point at which the solid, liquid, and gas phases can all exist in equilibrium simultaneously is called:
questions.explanation: The Triple Point is where solid, liquid, and gas coexist. For water: 0.01°C, 0.006 atm.

questions.subject: TH03
questions.text: According to Charles' Law, at constant pressure, the volume of a gas is:

options.text: Inversely proportional to its absolute temperature
Directly proportional to its absolute temperature
Proportional to the square of temperature

questions.explanation: Charles' Law: V ∝ T at constant P. V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂ (using absolute temperature).

questions.subject: TH03
questions.text: According to Boyle's Law, at constant temperature, the volume of a gas is:

options.text: Directly proportional to its absolute pressure
Inversely proportional to its absolute pressure
Proportional to the square of pressure

questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: V ∝ 1/P at constant T. P₁V₁ = P₂V₂.

questions.subject: TH03
questions.text: Why should the Ideal Gas Law not be used to determine the properties of steam?

options.text: Steam is always at high pressure
Steam's behavior is considerably more complex than an ideal gas, so steam tables (experimentally derived data) must be used instead
The Ideal Gas Law only works for perfect gases

questions.explanation: Steam deviates significantly from ideal gas behavior, especially near saturation. Steam tables provide experimentally accurate properties.

questions.subject: TH04
questions.text: In a process where no heat is transferred to or from the system, what term describes this condition?
questions.explanation: Adiabatic process: Q = 0 (no heat transfer). Temperature and pressure can change in adiabatic processes.

questions.subject: TH04
questions.text: For a turbine, the work output is typically expressed as the difference between which two values?
questions.explanation: Turbine work: W = h_in - h_out. The enthalpy drop across the turbine equals the mechanical work output.

questions.subject: TH04
questions.text: What does the term 'isenthalpic' describe in a thermodynamic process?
questions.explanation: Isenthalpic process: enthalpy remains constant. Throttling (through a valve) is an isenthalpic process.

questions.subject: TH04
questions.text: Which of the following is the correct expression for the work input to a pump?
questions.explanation: Pump work: W = h_out - h_in (positive value). Work is input to increase fluid pressure and enthalpy.

questions.subject: TH04
questions.text: Condensate depression is defined as the difference between which two temperatures?
questions.explanation: Condensate depression = saturation temperature minus actual condensate temperature. A few degrees prevents pump cavitation.

questions.subject: TH04
questions.text: A process that occurs at constant pressure is called:
questions.explanation: Isobaric process: P = constant. Boiler heat addition and condenser heat rejection are isobaric processes.

questions.subject: TH04
questions.text: A process that occurs at constant temperature is referred to as:
questions.explanation: Isothermal process: T = constant. Often occurs during condensation or with isothermal compression/expansion.

questions.subject: TH04
questions.text: What does the Second Law of Thermodynamics state about a heat engine operating in a cycle?

options.text: All heat can be converted to work
No engine, actual or ideal, can convert all the heat supplied into work; some heat must always be rejected
Heat only flows from cold to hot
The amount of entropy always decreases

questions.explanation: Second Law: No cyclic heat engine can achieve 100% efficiency. All engines must reject heat to the surroundings.

questions.subject: TH04
questions.text: The change in entropy of a system is defined as which of the following?

options.text: The heat added to the system multiplied by temperature
The heat transferred to or from the system divided by the absolute temperature at which the transfer occurs
The change in internal energy of the system

questions.explanation: Entropy change: ΔS = Q/T_abs. Total entropy increases in any real irreversible process (Second Law).

questions.subject: TH04
questions.text: In a convergent nozzle, what happens to the velocity and pressure of the fluid?
questions.explanation: In convergent nozzles: flow area decreases → velocity increases, pressure decreases (kinetic energy increases).

questions.subject: TH04
questions.text: Why is a few degrees of condensate subcooling desirable at the condenser hotwell?

options.text: To prevent cavitation in the condensate pumps

questions.explanation: Condensate depression prevents pump cavitation (vapor bubbles) but excessive depression wastes heat and increases corrosion.

questions.subject: TH04
questions.text: An ideal turbine or pump work process is described as isentropic because:

options.text: Friction is not considered, so there is no change in entropy of the working fluid

questions.explanation: Isentropic process: ΔS = 0 (reversible). Real turbines/pumps add entropy through friction, reducing their efficiency.

questions.subject: TH04
questions.text: Turbine efficiency is defined as which of the following ratios?
questions.explanation: Turbine efficiency = Real work output / Ideal isentropic work. Real turbines are always less efficient than ideal.

questions.subject: TH05
questions.text: What are the five essential elements of a thermodynamic cycle?

options.text: A working fluid, an engine to convert heat to work, a heat source, a heat sink, and a device to move the working fluid
A compressor, turbine, pump, condenser, and boiler
Temperature, pressure, volume, enthalpy, and entropy
Input, output, efficiency, work, and heat

questions.explanation: The five essential elements of a thermodynamic cycle are: a working fluid, an engine to convert heat to work, a heat source, a heat sink, and a device to move the working fluid.

questions.subject: TH05
questions.text: The Carnot cycle consists of which four processes?

options.text: Two isothermal processes and two adiabatic (isentropic) processes
Two isochoric processes and two isobaric processes

questions.explanation: The Carnot cycle consists of two isothermal processes and two adiabatic (isentropic) processes, operating between two heat reservoirs.

questions.subject: TH05
questions.text: According to the Carnot efficiency equation, the efficiency of a Carnot cycle depends only on:

options.text: The absolute temperatures of the heat source and heat sink
The type of working fluid used
The size and design of the engine

questions.explanation: The Carnot efficiency depends only on the absolute temperatures of the heat source and heat sink: η = 1 - (Tcold/Thot).

questions.subject: TH05
questions.text: What are the two ways to increase the Carnot efficiency of a cycle?

options.text: Increase the temperature of the heat source or decrease the temperature of the heat sink
Increase the amount of working fluid and reduce system pressure
Use a more efficient turbine and condenser
Add more heat exchangers to the system

questions.explanation: Carnot efficiency increases by raising the heat source temperature or lowering the heat sink temperature, both of which increase the temperature ratio.

questions.subject: TH05
questions.text: What is the primary purpose of a Moisture Separator Reheater (MSR) in a steam cycle?

options.text: To reduce moisture in the steam entering the low-pressure turbine, protecting the final blading stages from wear
To improve overall cycle efficiency by increasing work output
To reduce the amount of feedwater heating required
To increase the condenser vacuum and lower rejection temperature

questions.explanation: The MSR protects turbine blades from erosion and damage caused by moisture, not to improve efficiency directly.

questions.subject: TH05
questions.text: How does feedwater heating (using extraction steam) affect cycle efficiency?

options.text: It increases cycle efficiency because less heat must be added by the heat source
It decreases efficiency because energy is extracted from the turbine
It has no effect on efficiency
It only improves reliability, not efficiency

questions.explanation: Feedwater heating improves efficiency by reducing the amount of heat that must be added in the boiler to achieve the desired cycle conditions.

questions.subject: TH05
questions.text: How does increasing condenser vacuum (lowering absolute condenser pressure) affect cycle efficiency?

options.text: It increases efficiency, because more work is extracted from the steam and less heat is rejected
It decreases efficiency due to higher pumping losses
It has no effect on efficiency
It increases efficiency only if feedwater heating is also used

questions.explanation: Lower condenser pressure increases the expansion ratio in the turbine, extracting more work per unit of steam and improving efficiency.

questions.subject: TH05
questions.text: What is heat rate, and how does it relate to plant efficiency?

options.text: Heat rate is the energy input (BTU) required per unit of electrical output (kW-hr); it is inversely related to efficiency
Heat rate is the temperature rise across the condenser
Heat rate is the amount of heat added by the boiler per hour
Heat rate is the same as efficiency expressed in different units

questions.explanation: Heat rate (BTU/kW-hr) is inversely proportional to efficiency. A lower heat rate indicates higher efficiency.
